content.text: A quality bar is only as useful as its enforcement mechanism. Platform libraries consumed by dozens of services can't rely on reviewer goodwill — every check that matters must be automated, and every automated check must be fast enough that contributors don't learn to skip it. This document defines the specific quality bars for inner-source contributions to GenAI platform tools: what is checked, how it is enforced, and what a reviewer is actually expected to evaluate during code review.
Yes — explicit sign-off required for security-sensitive changes
Why four gates rather than one?

Each gate catches a different class of defect at the cheapest point. Pre-commit catches formatting in milliseconds before a push. CI catches test failures before a human spends time reviewing. Code review catches design and correctness issues that automated tools miss. The security gate ensures a human with security context explicitly signs off — automated scans catch patterns, not intent.
once after cloning; after that every commit runs the hooks automatically.

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: v0.4.4
    hooks:
      - id: ruff
        args: [--fix]
      - id: ruff-format

  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-mypy
    rev: v1.10.0
    hooks:
      - id: mypy
        args: [--strict, --ignore-missing-imports]
        additional_dependencies: [pydantic, httpx]

  - repo: https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets
    rev: v1.4.0
    hooks:
      - id: detect-secrets
        args: [--baseline, .secrets.baseline]

  - repo: https://github.com/compilerla/conventional-pre-commit
    rev: v3.2.0
    hooks:
      - id: conventional-pre-commit
        stages: [commit-msg]
        args: [feat, fix, docs, test, chore, refactor, perf, ci, build]

  - repo: local
    hooks:
      - id: no-debug-print
        name: No bare print() in src/
        language: pygrep
        entry: '^[^#]*print\('
        files: ^src/
        exclude: /__init__\.py$

Coverage is measured on unit tests only — contract and integration tests are excluded from the coverage calculation because they hit external APIs that may be flaky. The 85% line coverage floor is enforced by
. New files that lower coverage below the floor block merge.

# .github/workflows/quality.yml (excerpt)
- name: Unit tests with coverage
  run: |
    pytest tests/unit/ \
      --cov=src \
      --cov-report=xml \
      --cov-report=term-missing \
      --cov-fail-under=85 \
      --cov-config=.coveragerc

- name: Upload coverage to Codecov
  uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
  with:
    file: coverage.xml
    fail_ci_if_error: false   # coverage upload failure doesn't block merge

# .coveragerc
[run]
source = src
omit =
    src/**/__init__.py
    src/**/py.typed

[report]
exclude_lines =
    pragma: no cover
    if TYPE_CHECKING:
    raise NotImplementedError
    @abstractmethod

# .github/workflows/quality.yml (excerpt)
- name: Check dependencies for known CVEs (OSV)
  uses: google/osv-scanner-action@v1
  with:
    scan-args: |-
      --lockfile=requirements.txt
      --lockfile=requirements-dev.txt
  continue-on-error: false   # hard fail on HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs

- name: Check for outdated pinned versions
  run: |
    pip install pip-audit
    pip-audit --require-hashes -r requirements.txt \
      --ignore-vuln GHSA-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx   # known false positive

# scripts/check-changelog.py — run in CI to verify CHANGELOG is updated
import sys, re

UNRELEASED_SECTION = re.compile(r"## \[Unreleased\]")
CONTENT_UNDER_UNRELEASED = re.compile(
    r"## \[Unreleased\].*?(?=## \[)", re.DOTALL
)

with open("CHANGELOG.md") as f:
    content = f.read()

if not UNRELEASED_SECTION.search(content):
    print("ERROR: CHANGELOG.md has no [Unreleased] section.")
    sys.exit(1)

match = CONTENT_UNDER_UNRELEASED.search(content)
if not match or len(match.group(0).strip().splitlines()) < 3:
    print("ERROR: [Unreleased] section appears empty. Add a changelog entry.")
    sys.exit(1)

print("CHANGELOG.md check passed.")

Automated checks verify what can be verified automatically. Code review is for the rest: correctness, design consistency, and the properties that are hard to specify in a linter rule. TCs use a structured review checklist rather than open-ended feedback to keep reviews consistent across contributors.

## PR review checklist — Trusted Committer

### Correctness
- [ ] Does the implementation match the stated intent in the issue?
- [ ] Are edge cases handled (empty inputs, None, very large inputs, concurrent calls)?
- [ ] Are error messages actionable — do they tell the caller what to fix?
- [ ] Does the code behave correctly under retry (idempotent operations)?

### Interface consistency
- [ ] Does the new code follow the LLMProvider / Scorer protocol exactly?
- [ ] Are new public names (classes, functions, exceptions) consistent with existing naming conventions?
- [ ] Is the interface the minimum necessary — no unnecessary parameters or return fields?
- [ ] Is backwards compatibility preserved, or is there a migration path documented?

### Test quality
- [ ] Do the tests assert on observable behaviour, not on internal state?
- [ ] Does each test have a clear failure message when it fails?
- [ ] Is the happy path tested? The empty/null case? The error path?
- [ ] Are tests fast and deterministic (no sleep, no non-deterministic ordering)?

### Security (for changes touching auth, secrets, or external calls)
- [ ] No secrets, tokens, or internal hostnames in the code or test fixtures.
- [ ] External inputs are validated before use.
- [ ] No subprocess calls with user-controlled strings (shell injection risk).
- [ ] LLM inputs that come from user data are labelled as potentially adversarial.

### Documentation
- [ ] Public functions have a one-line docstring stating what they do and what they return.
- [ ] Complex algorithms or non-obvious choices have an inline comment explaining WHY.
- [ ] CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] section is updated.
- [ ] docs/adapters.md or equivalent is updated if the public API changes.

The TC Council explicitly defines how review comments should be written. An inner-source program lives or dies on whether contributors feel that feedback is helpful and respectful. The following norms are binding for all reviewers:
Prefix with 'Blocking:' — must be resolved before approval
'Blocking: This passes the raw user string to subprocess.run() without validation — command injection risk.'
Prefix with 'Suggestion:' — contributor may accept or explain why not
'Suggestion: You could use `lru_cache` here to avoid a Vault round-trip on every call. Up to you.'
Prefix with 'Question:' — does not block, but needs an answer
'Question: Is this method expected to be called concurrently? If so, is the shared dict access safe?'
Prefix with 'Nit:' — reviewer preference, not required to change
'Nit: I would name this `provider_name` rather than `pname` for readability.'
Plain text — acknowledging good work matters
'This test structure is clean — I'll use this as a reference for other adapters.'

GenAI platform libraries have additional quality concerns that general software review checklists miss. Every PR that touches a component that processes user inputs, calls an LLM, or handles credentials must be reviewed against these criteria.

Any adapter or middleware that concatenates user-supplied strings into a prompt must document the injection surface and either sanitise the input or explicitly note in the docstring that the caller is responsible for sanitisation.

New code paths that call LLM APIs must pass through the metered client so token costs are attributed to the correct service. Bypassing the LLMClient to call the provider SDK directly is a blocking review issue.
Hard-coded model names are a blocking review issue. Model references must come from configuration or the router — not from string literals in business logic.

Retry logic must distinguish transient errors (429, 502, 503) from permanent errors (400, 401, 404). Retrying a 400 wastes quota. The middleware pipeline's retry logic is authoritative — adapters should not implement their own.

No adapter may accept an API key as a constructor parameter in production code. Credentials must be resolved by the CredentialResolver. Test fixtures may use environment variables; never string literals.
Contract Testing as a Quality Bar

Unit tests verify that a class does what its implementation says. Contract tests verify that a class satisfies the protocol that consumers depend on. For adapter contributions, a contract test is mandatory — it is the only automated check that guarantees a new
will work as a drop-in replacement for the existing

# tests/contract/adapter_contract.py
# Shared contract test suite — all adapters must pass every test in this module.

import pytest
from genai_sdk.core.protocol import LLMProvider, CompletionRequest, CompletionResponse

class AdapterContractTests:
    """
    Mixin for adapter contract tests.
    Subclass this and set `self.adapter` in setUp/fixture.
    """

    def test_returns_completion_response(self):
        req  = CompletionRequest(user="Say hello.", max_tokens=10)
        resp = self.adapter.complete(req)
        assert isinstance(resp, CompletionResponse)

    def test_response_has_non_empty_text(self):
        req  = CompletionRequest(user="Say hello.", max_tokens=20)
        resp = self.adapter.complete(req)
        assert len(resp.text.strip()) > 0

    def test_response_has_positive_token_counts(self):
        req  = CompletionRequest(user="Say hello.", max_tokens=20)
        resp = self.adapter.complete(req)
        assert resp.input_tokens > 0
        assert resp.output_tokens > 0

    def test_system_prompt_is_honoured(self):
        req = CompletionRequest(
            system="Respond only with the word PONG.",
            user="PING",
            max_tokens=10,
        )
        resp = self.adapter.complete(req)
        assert "pong" in resp.text.lower()

    def test_max_tokens_is_respected(self):
        req  = CompletionRequest(user="Count from 1 to 1000.", max_tokens=5)
        resp = self.adapter.complete(req)
        assert resp.output_tokens <= 10   # allow small overage for BPE tokenisation

    def test_supports_model_returns_bool(self):
        assert isinstance(self.adapter.supports_model("some-model"), bool)

    def test_count_tokens_is_positive(self):
        count = self.adapter.count_tokens("Hello, world!")
        assert count > 0

    def test_provider_name_is_set(self):
        req  = CompletionRequest(user="Hi", max_tokens=5)
        resp = self.adapter.complete(req)
        assert resp.provider == self.adapter.name

At the end of CI, a summary bot posts a quality scorecard comment on the PR. This gives contributors an at-a-glance view of where they stand before a TC reviews, and gives TCs a structured summary to check before opening the diff.

# scripts/post-pr-scorecard.py
# Called at the end of CI; posts a Markdown comment on the PR via GitHub API.

import httpx, os, json

def get_coverage() -> float:
    with open("coverage.json") as f:
        return json.load(f)["totals"]["percent_covered"]

def get_semgrep_findings() -> int:
    with open("semgrep-results.json") as f:
        return len(json.load(f).get("results", []))

def check_changelog() -> bool:
    with open("CHANGELOG.md") as f:
        return "[Unreleased]" in f.read()

coverage      = get_coverage()
security_findings = get_semgrep_findings()
changelog_ok  = check_changelog()
dco_ok        = os.environ.get("DCO_STATUS") == "passed"

status = lambda ok: "✅" if ok else "❌"

comment = f"""
## Quality Scorecard

| Check                | Result                          |
|----------------------|---------------------------------|
| Unit test coverage   | {status(coverage >= 85)} {coverage:.1f}% (min 85%)   |
| Security scan        | {status(security_findings == 0)} {security_findings} finding(s) |
| CHANGELOG.md updated | {status(changelog_ok)}                           |
| DCO sign-off         | {status(dco_ok)}                                 |

{'✅ All automated gates passed. Ready for TC review.' if all([coverage >= 85, security_findings == 0, changelog_ok, dco_ok]) else '❌ One or more gates failed. Fix the issues above before requesting review.'}
"""

pr_number = os.environ["PR_NUMBER"]
repo      = os.environ["GITHUB_REPOSITORY"]
token     = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]

httpx.post(
    f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/issues/{pr_number}/comments",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
    json={"body": comment},
)

Quality bars are not fixed. The TC Council reviews them quarterly and updates them when the existing bars no longer reflect the current threat model or codebase maturity. The process for raising or lowering a bar:
A TC opens a GitHub Discussion proposing the change with a rationale (e.g. 'raising coverage floor from 85% to 90% because we now have integration test infrastructure that makes this realistic').
A 5-day comment period allows contributors to raise concerns — particularly if a proposed bar would retroactively invalidate existing contributions.
TC Council votes; a simple majority passes.
The winning change is applied to the CI pipeline and CONTRIBUTING.md in the same PR.
The platform Slack channel is notified with the effective date — bars that change mid-sprint for active contributors require a 2-week notice period.
High enough to catch most regressions; low enough that adapter stubs don't require 100% mock coverage of SDK internals
Any high or critical finding is a merge blocker; MEDIUM findings generate a TODO that must be resolved within 30 days
One reviewer catches obvious issues; two catches subtle ones — the marginal review cost is low compared to the bug cost
Adapters are hot-swappable; a contract test proves the swap is safe without full integration testing
Consumers rely on changelogs to decide whether to upgrade; missing entries create silent breaking changes

Quality bars work when they are automated, documented, and consistently applied. The four-gate pipeline — pre-commit, CI, code review, security sign-off — catches defects at the cheapest point for each class of issue. The TC review checklist standardises what human reviewers evaluate, preventing inconsistency across contributors and reviewers. GenAI-specific bars — prompt injection surface, token attribution, model coupling, retry discipline, and credential handling — address the failure modes that general review checklists miss. The result is a platform library suite where contributors from any team can make changes with confidence that the review process will catch what matters and not waste their time on what does not.
